body_txt: In many militaries, sufficient degrees of badassery or competence can get someone promoted above where their background would usually allow. Sometimes they don't want to, preferring the relative freedom of lower rank combined with behind the scenes influence. The potential promotee will explain that command comes with too many headaches, too much responsibility, not enough action, or a dozen other reasons, but they all boil down to being happy where they are. They are often deliberately averting The Peter Principle by refusing to be promoted out of their range of competence. This often leads to becoming an Almighty Janitor . Another reason for this is simple pragmatism. Someone may decide they want to retire or separate from the military and therefore aren't interested in being promoted, because accepting the promotion also means incurring an additional service commitment of anywhere from three to five years where you are not allowed to separate or retire (except for medical reasons). Promotions get turned down fairly regularly for this reason by personnel who have made up their mind to leave. Reasons to refuse a promotion exist even in Real Life . A workplace's rules may be written in such a way that being promoted has the potential to earn substantially more money in the long run, but for the first two or three years actually brings in less. The promotion from hourly worker to salaried management includes a raise, but not a large enough one to make up for the fact that you can no longer bill your overtime work. A new title may come with a complete reset of all acquired perks based on your years of service, meaning that declining promotions offered in the last years before your retirement will benefit your pension more than accepting them. A darker version of this trope has someone refuse a promotion only to discover that their superiors have now inferred, rightly or wrongly, that they don't (or didn't at the time) have the drive to advance and thus are no longer allowed to . Often part of a Meaningful Job or Cushy Job conflict. Contrast Up Through the Ranks (a veteran enlisted man gains a commission as an officer), Kicked Upstairs (someone is promoted to get them into a position of less power), and Rank Up ( Super-Trope for plain old promotion). Compare Dismissing a Compliment , Deliberate Under-Performance , Gracefully Demoted , and Medal of Dishonor when they don't like the promotion because of shady reasons behind it, who may or may not take the new position anyway. Passed-Over Promotion deals with somebody else being promoted in their place. People in this trope may be well aware of the risk of becoming a Desk Jockey .
